topic: A person's manuscript is rejected by their dream publisher
raw_output:
[story 1]

The thick envelope, cool and unforgiving in my hand, felt like a physical insult. I stared at the embossed seal of 'Aethelred & Sons,' the name that had been the pinnacle of my literary aspirations for five years. Inside, the single, curt note read: "Not the right fit." My jaw tightened until my molars ached. I crumpled the paper, the sound a sharp, tearing noise in the otherwise quiet apartment. I paced the worn rug, my footsteps heavy and uneven. Every corner of the room seemed to vibrate with a simmering heat. I slammed my fist onto the desk, rattling the inkwell. "Not the right fit? After all the sleepless nights, the revisions, the sheer *soul* poured into that manuscript?" I muttered the words, my voice dangerously low, a tremor running through my hands. I snatched up my laptop, the screen illuminating my face with a harsh, angry glare. I needed to burn this rejection, to dismantle this crushing disappointment piece by piece.

[story 2]

I reread the rejection letter three times, my eyes scanning the typeface as if searching for some hidden flaw, some deliberate slight. A slow, burning flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I stood by the window, the city lights blurring through the condensation on the glass, mirroring the chaotic swirl inside my chest. It wasn't just a rejection; it felt like a fundamental dismissal of everything I had built. I walked to the bookshelf, gripping the spine of a well-loved novel so tightly my knuckles turned white. A sharp, involuntary breath escaped me—a sound halfway between a gasp and a snarl. "They simply don't *get* it," I whispered to the empty room, the conviction in my tone laced with bitter incredulity. I spun around, kicking lightly at a stack of old manuscripts, the sudden movement fueled by a fierce, restless energy. This wasn't over. This was an injustice demanding a furious response.

[story 3]

The phone call ended with a dull, final click that echoed in the sudden silence of my study. I dropped the receiver onto the mahogany desk with unnecessary force, the vibration seeming to shake the very foundations of the room. I didn't move for a full minute, just stood there, breathing in ragged, shallow bursts. When I finally moved, it was with deliberate, sharp motions. I walked over to the framed manuscript—the physical embodiment of my ambition—and traced the cover with a finger, pressing down hard enough to leave a slight indentation. A wave of hot frustration washed over me, making my vision swim momentarily. "Unbelievable," I hissed, the word tasting like ash. I grabbed a nearby pen and began savagely attacking a blank page, scribbling furious, illegible lines until the paper tore under the pressure. Every stroke was an act of defiance against the cold, dismissive words I had just received.
